headline: Teslas European sales slump for fifth month
summary: STORY: Teslas new car sales in Europe fell just under 28% in May from a year earlier.That was despite sales of fully-electric vehicles rising over 27% in the region.Thats according to trade data released Wednesday (June 25) by the European Automobile Manufacturers Association.Teslas European sales have now fallen for five straight months as customers switch to cheaper Chinese EVs.Some potential buyers have also protested against Tesla CEO Elon Musks politics.Teslas European market share dropped to just 1.2% in May from 1.8% a year ago.The U.S. EV makers revised Model Y has yet to show signs of reviving the brands fortunes.It was aimed at revamping the companys aging model range.Particularly when traditional automakers and Chinese rivals have launched EVs at a fast pace amid trade tensions.Chinese manufacturers kept up their strong growth in Europe last month despite EU tariffs on the countrys EVs.They doubled market share to 5.9%, according to separate data released Tuesday.BYD registered nearly as many vehicles as Tesla in May, after outselling it in April.

headline: The Week in Numbers: tariff deadline, Tesla troubles
summary: STORY: From why there could be bad news in the post for some nations, to more pain for Tesla, this is the Week in Numbers20% is the tariff set for Vietnamese goods by Donald Trump, days before his July 9 deadline for trade deals.The number helped cool tensions, as it was less than half the rate the country had faced without an agreement.It may be a different story for other nations, with Trump saying he would start sending out letters just telling them what their rate is:“Its just much easier. We have, you know, far more than 170 countries. And how many deals can you make? And you can make good deals, but theyre very much more complicated.”Up to 34.9% is the tariff China is imposing on EU brandy.European cognac makers say they’re collateral damage in a broader dispute over import levies imposed by Brussels on Chinese EVs.Beijing is still pushing for steps to ease the European auto duties.13.5% was the slide in Tesla sales over the second quarter, marking its second straight period of decline.The brand appears to be suffering from an aging model lineup, and boss Elon Musk’s contentious role in politics.Tesla now faces an uphill task to avoid a second year of falling sales.$11 billion is the one-off loss set to be booked by Renault as it writes down the value of its stake in struggling Japanese partner Nissan.The move marks a further loosening of ties between the pair after a two-decade alliance.Nissan has battled a series of crises in recent years, and now faces slumping sales.And nearly 4% is the share of Microsoft workers facing the axe.The tech giant is trying to rein in costs amid massive spending on AI infrastructure.Many managers will look vulnerable, with Microsoft planning to reduce the number of layers in its vast workforce.
